attributes.stem: A 50-year-old pre-menopausal woman presents for a review of her bone mineral density scan. Over the last two years, she has experienced several metacarpal fractures that did not appear to be associated with undue trauma. Her history includes asthma for which she uses high-dose budesonide/formoterol inhaler regularly. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. She has had multiple recent asthma exacerbations for which she has required hospital admissions and prolonged weaning courses of prednisolone ≥7.5mg daily dose for the last three months. On physical examination, she is kyphotic and has some wheezing on chest auscultation. The examination is otherwise unremarkable. A DEXA scan shows a T-score of -2.0 in the lumbar spine and -1.5 in the hip.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
Whilst the patient's T-scores are within the osteopenic range, she has a history of prolonged and high dose glucocorticoid exposure (defined as prednisolone ≥7.5mg/day or dose equivalent for three months). This increases her risk of future fractures, and treatment is indicated for glucocorticoid-induced osteopenia i.e., T-scores ≤-1.5. It is important to note that whilst minimal trauma, metacarpal fractures are not considered diagnostic of osteopororsis, only distal radial, hip, and vertebral fractures. For this patient, pharmacological therapy is indicated to reduce the risk of future fractures and repeat DEXA scan in 3 years alone is inappropriate.

Bisphosphonates (Risedronate) and Denosumab are reasonable first-line options for the management of osteoporosis. However, in cases of glucocorticoid-induced or related osteoporosis, bisphosphonates are the preferred first-line therapy (and approved on the PBS as such).

Risedronate (bisphosphonates), is a potent inhibitor of osteoclasts - not only by interfering with the normal functioning of osteoclasts, but also by inducing apoptosis. Denosumab is a monoclonal antibody against RANK-ligand, which plays a crucial role in regulating osteoclast development and activity - reduces osteoclast formation, differentiation and promotes apoptosis. Bisphosphonates are preferred over denosumab for osteoporosis associated with glucocorticoid use, and especially in patients with known vertebral fractures, due to emerging evidence of an increased incidence of multiple spontaneous vertebral fractures when denosumab is discontinued or doses delayed without an alternative agent in the interim. Whilst bisphosphonates have a long skeletal half-life i.e. their beneficial effects can remain for years after ceasing therapy, cessation of denosumab results in a much quicker rise in bone turnover and consequent loss of bone mineral density. Denosumab use is also only recommended for ten years, meaning it is inappropriate in people whose life expectancy exceeds this, which this patient's probably does.

Teriparatide, an analogue of human parathyroid hormone, helps with osteoporosis by increasing bone formation. Practical challenges include the need for daily subcutaneous injections. Furthermore, teriparatide has been linked to an increased risk of osteosarcoma in animal studies (though no evidence of such in humans has been observed).

Romosozumab, is another anabolic agent which reduces bone resorption whilst increasing bone formation. It is a monoclonal antibody and its mechanism of action is via sclerostin inhibition.

In Australia, the use of teriparatide and romosozumab are only listed for 'severe established osteoporosis' on the pharmaceutical benefits scheme, with many stipulations including being at very high risk of fracture, T-score ≤-3.0, having two or more fractures due to minimal trauma, and experiencing at least one symptomatic new fracture after at least 12 months continuous therapy with an anti-resorptive agent at adequate doses.
learningPoints: Bisphosphonates are the first-line treatment option in patients with osteoporosis/osteoporotic fractures associated with steroid use
references: Compston J, Cooper A, Cooper C, Gittoes N, Gregson C, Harvey N, et al. UK clinical guideline for the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis. Archives of osteoporosis [Internet]. 2017;12(1):43. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/28425085

The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management in postmenopausal women and men over 50 years of age. 2nd ed. East Melbourne, VIC: RACGP; 2017.

The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Osteoporosis management and fracture prevention in post-menopausal women and men > 50 years of age – Summary of recommendations [Internet]. RACGP. 2024 [cited 2025 Apr 14]. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/key-racgp-guidelines/view-all-racgp-guidelines/osteoporosis/summary-of-recommendations
